narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the typical student's level. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and test scores, estimated a 90.8% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed on the bar and graduate strong. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the average of about 5.5 and strongly signaled higher bar risk compared to most students. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the typical 5.7, was the next most influential, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 27, significantly lower than the average 36.7, further pushed the model toward predicting failure, as higher scores are common among those who pass the bar. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, performing in line with successful students. Your middle family income bracket had a small influence toward higher risk, while planning to attend full-time—shared by over 90% of students—provided a minor boost toward success. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your lower deciles and LSAT stood out as bigger hurdles than average. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your high school or early college grades through additional coursework if possible, and exploring programs that support students from similar backgrounds in building bar readiness. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing; your perseverance as a Black woman in law will inspire others.
